questions.prompt_html: If f'(x) = 0 throughout an interval [a, b], prove that f(x) is constant on the interval.
questions.prompt_text: If f'(x) = 0 throughout an interval [a, b], prove that f(x) is constant on the interval.
questions.solution_html:
If a < x ≤ b, the mean-value theorem assures us that f(x) - f(a) = f'(t)(x - a) for some t in (a, x).
Since f'(t) = 0, we must have f(x) = f(a). This is true for all x in [a, b].

questions.prompt_text: Define carefully what is meant by: The function V(x,y) is a Liapunov function in a domain D (containing the fixed point at the origin) for the autonomous system associated with the vector field F = P(x,y) i + Q(j, x,y). Assume that P and Q have continuous partial derivatives in D.
questions.solution_html:
A function V(x, y) is called a Liapunov function for an autonomous system associated with vector field F if
(i)V(x,y) has continuous partials in D
(ii)V(x,y) is positive definite in D [that is, V(x,y) > 0 for all (x,y) ≠ (0, 0) and V(x,y) = 0 
if and only if (x, y) = (0, 0)]
(iii)= (∇ V)∙ F ≤  0 in D (that is,  is negative semi definite in D)
